source: Study guide derived from RIC-3, RBR-4, RIC-9 and the official Basic question bank.

modules.title: Regulations and Policies
modules.description: The legal and administrative rules behind the Basic Qualification.

sections.title: What the Basic Qualification authorizes
sections.content: Authority to operate in the amateur radio service is issued through the Amateur Radio Operator Certificate with Basic Qualification. The Basic exam is the entry point. A pass is 70 percent, and a score of 80 percent or higher grants Basic with Honours privileges, including access to amateur bands below 30 MHz under RBR-4.

sections.title: Exam facts
points: The Basic examination has 100 questions.
The exam is built by drawing one question from each of the 100 RIC-3 Basic topic areas.
The pass mark is 70 percent.
A score of 80 percent or higher grants Basic with Honours privileges.
RIC-3 specifies no fixed time limit; most exams are completed within one hour and normally not more than two hours.
Examinations are closed book.

sections.title: Rules, privileges and limits
sections.content: Regulation questions often turn on exact wording. Know which document gives authority, who administers the Act, what must be retained or produced, when a change of address must be reported, what transmissions are prohibited, and which qualifications unlock which privileges. Basic operators have access to amateur bands above 30 MHz, may use 250 W DC input power, may assemble commercially available transmitter kits of professional design, may reprogram equipment by computer program for amateur-band operation, and may use repeaters. Advanced and Morse qualifications add further privileges.

sections.title: Qualification summary
rows: All amateur bands above 30 MHz; 250 W DC input; commercial kit assembly; computer reprogramming for amateur bands; repeater operation.
Basic privileges plus access to all amateur bands below 30 MHz.
Below-30 MHz access, 1000 W DC input, build/modify transmitters, establish repeaters and club stations, remote control fixed stations.

sections.title: Call signs and identification
sections.content: A Canadian call sign is assigned using the prefix for the operator's Canadian address. Station identification rules, prefix areas, special-event prefixes, and foreign operation rules are frequent exam targets. For call sign questions, identify whether the question is asking about prefix area, suffix eligibility, station identification timing, special events, or operation by visitors.

fundamentals.heading: How to think about regulation questions
fundamentals.body: Regulation questions are not trying to test radio theory. They test whether you know who is allowed to do what, under which qualification, and with which limits. Read these questions like legal rules: find the actor, the permission or prohibition, and the exact condition. If two answers sound technically reasonable, choose the one that matches the certificate privilege or rule being asked.
fundamentals.example: If a question asks about operating below 30 MHz, the key distinction is Basic versus Basic with Honours, Morse, or Advanced.
fundamentals.trap: Do not choose an answer just because it is a real amateur radio term. Regulation distractors often use real terms in the wrong rule.

topic_lessons.title: Radio licences, applicability, eligibility of licence holder
topic_lessons.plain_language: This is about permission to operate. In Canada, the operator certificate is the key credential for amateur operation. Know who issues it, who must hold it, when information must be updated, and what inspectors can require.
topic_lessons.memorize: Memorize who has authority, what the certificate permits, what is prohibited, and any exact time, power, frequency, bandwidth, or qualification threshold.
topic_lessons.example: If the question asks about a change of address, look for the answer that matches the administrative duty, not a technical operating answer.
topic_lessons.trap: Do not confuse a station authorization concept with the operator certificate.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain radio licences, applicability, eligibility of licence holder without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Content restrictions: non-superfluous, profanity, secret code, music, non-commercial
topic_lessons.plain_language: Amateur radio is for self-training, intercommunication, and technical investigation, not broadcasting, secret messages, music, business traffic, or paid communications.
topic_lessons.memorize: Memorize who has authority, what the certificate permits, what is prohibited, and any exact time, power, frequency, bandwidth, or qualification threshold.
topic_lessons.example: A message can be technically possible to send and still be prohibited because of its content or purpose.
topic_lessons.trap: The exam often offers an answer that sounds polite or useful but is still not allowed in amateur service.

topic_lessons.title: Emergency communications, simulated emergencies, communication with non-amateur stations
topic_lessons.plain_language: Emergency rules allow communication needed for immediate safety, but they do not allow false distress signals or careless simulated emergencies.
topic_lessons.memorize: Memorize who has authority, what the certificate permits, what is prohibited, and any exact time, power, frequency, bandwidth, or qualification threshold.
topic_lessons.example: In a real emergency, the goal is clear, brief communication that gets help.
topic_lessons.trap: Emergency exceptions are not permission for casual third-party or commercial traffic.

modules.title: Operating and Procedures
modules.description: On-air procedure, repeaters, simplex, CW, signal reports, Q signals and emergencies.

sections.title: Clear operating habits
sections.content: Good amateur procedure is concise, identifiable and courteous. Listen before transmitting, identify correctly, use standard phonetics when spelling, keep tuneups brief, use a dummy load where possible, and choose the appropriate mode and frequency for the contact. Repeater questions often test offset, courtesy tones, time-out timers, and why a short pause between transmissions matters.

sections.title: Common Q signals
columns: Meaning as a question or statement
rows: Is this frequency in use? / This frequency is in use.
Can you acknowledge receipt? / I acknowledge receipt.

sections.title: Emergency priorities
points: Emergency communications may involve non-amateur stations when immediate safety requires it.
A false or fraudulent distress signal is prohibited.
In an emergency, the communication should be brief, clear, and directed at getting assistance.
Simulated emergency tests must not mislead other stations into believing a real emergency exists.

fundamentals.heading: How to think about operating procedure
fundamentals.body: Operating procedure is about being clear, identifiable, brief, and courteous on the air. Most answers follow from that idea. Listen first, identify properly, keep tests short, use standard phonetics and signal reports, and give emergency traffic priority. When a question mentions a repeater, think about offsets, time-out timers, courtesy pauses, and sharing the channel.
fundamentals.example: A short pause before replying on a repeater lets others break in and helps avoid time-out timer problems.
fundamentals.trap: Do not treat ham radio like a private phone call. Identification and shared-channel courtesy matter.

modules.title: Station Assembly, Practice and Safety
modules.description: How station blocks work and how to avoid electrical, tower and RF hazards.

sections.title: Station block diagrams
sections.content: Most station-layout questions ask what a stage does or where it belongs. Transmitters generate, modulate, amplify and filter RF before it reaches the antenna system. Receivers select, amplify, mix, detect/demodulate and recover audio or data. Power supplies convert AC or DC into regulated voltages, while accessories such as keyers, microphones, TNCs, filters, meters and tuners support the operating mode.

sections.title: Safety rules
points: Disconnect power before servicing equipment.
High-voltage capacitors can remain charged after power is removed; discharge them safely.
Use the correct fuse rating. Never install a larger fuse to stop nuisance blowing.
Grounding reduces shock risk and helps control static and lightning effects.
Keep people away from energized antennas and observe RF exposure limits.
Tower and antenna work requires fall protection, safe distances from power lines, and weather awareness.

fundamentals.heading: How to think about station and safety questions
fundamentals.body: Station questions become easier when you picture signal flow. A transmitter creates and modulates RF, amplifies it, filters it, and sends it to the antenna. A receiver selects a signal, converts or detects it, then recovers audio or data. Safety questions ask what hazard exists and what action removes the risk: disconnect power, discharge capacitors, use correct fuses, ground properly, stay away from power lines, and respect RF exposure limits.
fundamentals.example: A regulated power supply normally rectifies AC, filters it, then regulates the output voltage.
fundamentals.trap: Never solve a fuse problem by installing a larger fuse. The fuse protects wiring and equipment from excess current.

modules.title: Circuit Components
modules.description: The parts used in amateur radio circuits and what they do.

sections.title: Component recognition
sections.content: Component questions usually test a device's purpose, terminals or behaviour. Diodes conduct mainly in one direction and are used for rectification and switching. Bipolar transistors are current-controlled devices with emitter, base and collector. Field-effect transistors are voltage-controlled devices with source, gate and drain. Vacuum tubes such as triodes use cathode, grid and plate. Amplifiers increase voltage, current or power, but can distort or oscillate if biased or driven incorrectly.

fundamentals.heading: How to think about components
fundamentals.body: Component questions usually ask what a part does, how it is controlled, or what its terminals are called. A diode mainly conducts one way. A bipolar transistor is current-controlled. A field-effect transistor is voltage-controlled. A triode vacuum tube uses a grid to control current. Resistor colour questions are memorization plus careful place value.
fundamentals.example: For a BJT, remember emitter, base, collector. For an FET, remember source, gate, drain.
fundamentals.trap: Do not mix transistor terminal names. The exam often uses terminal names as distractors.

topic_lessons.title: Amplifier fundamentals
topic_lessons.plain_language: This topic focuses on amplifier fundamentals. For component questions, identify the device, the terminals, the direction or control mechanism, and the normal job of the component in a circuit.
topic_lessons.memorize: Memorize each component's job, terminals, and control method.
topic_lessons.example: Before answering, restate the question in your own words and identify the one concept being tested.
topic_lessons.trap: Distractors often sound familiar but answer a different topic.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain amplifier fundamentals without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Diode fundamentals
topic_lessons.plain_language: A diode mainly conducts in one direction, so it is useful for rectification, switching, and protection.
topic_lessons.memorize: Memorize each component's job, terminals, and control method.
topic_lessons.example: Rectifiers use diodes to turn AC into pulsating DC.
topic_lessons.trap: A diode is not a gain device like a transistor.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain diode fundamentals without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Bipolar transistor fundamentals
topic_lessons.plain_language: Transistors control a larger current using a smaller input signal. BJTs use base current; FETs use gate voltage.
topic_lessons.memorize: Memorize each component's job, terminals, and control method.
topic_lessons.example: BJT terminals: emitter, base, collector. FET terminals: source, gate, drain.
topic_lessons.trap: The exam often tests terminal names directly.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain bipolar transistor fundamentals without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Field-effect transistor fundamentals
topic_lessons.plain_language: Transistors control a larger current using a smaller input signal. BJTs use base current; FETs use gate voltage.
topic_lessons.memorize: Memorize each component's job, terminals, and control method.
topic_lessons.example: BJT terminals: emitter, base, collector. FET terminals: source, gate, drain.
topic_lessons.trap: The exam often tests terminal names directly.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain field-effect transistor fundamentals without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.plain_language: This topic focuses on triode vacuum tube fundamentals. For component questions, identify the device, the terminals, the direction or control mechanism, and the normal job of the component in a circuit.
topic_lessons.memorize: Memorize each component's job, terminals, and control method.
topic_lessons.example: Before answering, restate the question in your own words and identify the one concept being tested.

topic_lessons.plain_language: Resistor colour codes are a compact way to print resistance value and tolerance on the part.
topic_lessons.memorize: Memorize each component's job, terminals, and control method.
topic_lessons.example: Black is 0, brown 1, red 2, orange 3, yellow 4, green 5, blue 6, violet 7, grey 8, white 9.
topic_lessons.trap: Do not confuse digit bands with multiplier or tolerance bands.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain resistor colour codes, tolerances, temperature coefficient without looking at the answer choices?

modules.title: Basic Electronics and Theory
modules.description: The formulas and concepts behind voltage, current, resistance, power and resonance.

sections.title: Solving calculation questions
sections.content: Start by identifying the unknown. Ohm's law is E = I x R, so I = E / R and R = E / I. Power can be found with P = E x I, P = I squared x R, or P = E squared / R. Keep units consistent before calculating. Many wrong answers result from mixing milliamps with amps, kilohms with ohms, or using the right formula in the wrong direction.

sections.title: Resonance and reactance
points: Inductive reactance rises as frequency rises.
Capacitive reactance falls as frequency rises.
Resonance occurs when inductive and capacitive reactance are equal in magnitude.
A tuned circuit can select or reject a range of frequencies.

fundamentals.heading: How to think about electronics theory
fundamentals.body: Theory questions are easiest if you slow down and write the relationship first. Ohm's law is E = I x R. Power is P = E x I, and related formulas can be derived from Ohm's law. Convert units before calculating: milliamps to amps, kilohms to ohms, and megahertz to hertz only when needed. For AC concepts, focus on directional relationships: inductive reactance rises with frequency, capacitive reactance falls with frequency.
fundamentals.example: If E = 12 V and R = 6 ohms, I = E / R = 2 A. The units tell you whether the answer is plausible.
fundamentals.trap: A correct-looking number can be wrong if you forgot a metric prefix such as milli, kilo, or mega.

topic_lessons.title: Metric prefixes: pico, micro, milli, centi, kilo, mega, giga
topic_lessons.plain_language: Metric prefixes move the decimal point. Kilo is thousand, mega is million, milli is thousandth, and micro is millionth.
topic_lessons.memorize: Memorize formulas, unit prefixes, and how quantities move when frequency or circuit values change.
topic_lessons.example: 4.7 k ohms is 4700 ohms; 500 mA is 0.5 A.
topic_lessons.trap: Most wrong calculation answers come from prefix conversion errors.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain metric prefixes: pico, micro, milli, centi, kilo, mega, giga without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Concepts of current, voltage, conductor, insulator, resistance
topic_lessons.plain_language: Voltage is electrical pressure, current is flow, and resistance opposes flow. Ohm's law connects them: E = I x R.
topic_lessons.memorize: Memorize formulas, unit prefixes, and how quantities move when frequency or circuit values change.
topic_lessons.example: If E = 12 V and R = 6 ohms, I = 12 / 6 = 2 A.
topic_lessons.trap: Convert milliamps to amps before using the formula.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain concepts of current, voltage, conductor, insulator, resistance without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Concepts of energy and power, open and short circuits
topic_lessons.plain_language: Power is the rate of using or delivering energy. In DC exam problems, P = E x I is the most direct formula.
topic_lessons.memorize: Memorize formulas, unit prefixes, and how quantities move when frequency or circuit values change.
topic_lessons.example: 13.8 V at 10 A is 138 W.
topic_lessons.trap: Power and energy are related but not the same thing.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain concepts of energy and power, open and short circuits without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Ohm's law: single resistors
topic_lessons.plain_language: Voltage is electrical pressure, current is flow, and resistance opposes flow. Ohm's law connects them: E = I x R.
topic_lessons.memorize: Memorize formulas, unit prefixes, and how quantities move when frequency or circuit values change.
topic_lessons.example: If E = 12 V and R = 6 ohms, I = 12 / 6 = 2 A.
topic_lessons.trap: Convert milliamps to amps before using the formula.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain ohm's law: single resistors without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Series and parallel resistors
topic_lessons.plain_language: Series resistors add directly. Parallel resistance is less than the smallest branch, and equal resistors in parallel divide by the number of branches.
topic_lessons.memorize: Memorize formulas, unit prefixes, and how quantities move when frequency or circuit values change.
topic_lessons.example: Two 100 ohm resistors in parallel equal 50 ohms.
topic_lessons.trap: Do not add parallel resistors as if they were in series.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain series and parallel resistors without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Power law, resistor power dissipation
topic_lessons.plain_language: Power is the rate of using or delivering energy. In DC exam problems, P = E x I is the most direct formula.
topic_lessons.memorize: Memorize formulas, unit prefixes, and how quantities move when frequency or circuit values change.
topic_lessons.example: 13.8 V at 10 A is 138 W.
topic_lessons.trap: Power and energy are related but not the same thing.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain power law, resistor power dissipation without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: AC, sinewave, frequency, frequency units
topic_lessons.plain_language: This topic focuses on ac, sinewave, frequency, frequency units. For electronics theory questions, write the relationship first, keep units straight, and check whether the question asks for voltage, current, resistance, power, frequency, ratio, or a measurement concept.
topic_lessons.memorize: Memorize formulas, unit prefixes, and how quantities move when frequency or circuit values change.
topic_lessons.example: Before answering, restate the question in your own words and identify the one concept being tested.
topic_lessons.trap: Distractors often sound familiar but answer a different topic.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain ac, sinewave, frequency, frequency units without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Ratios, logarithms, decibels
topic_lessons.plain_language: Decibels compare ratios. For power, +3 dB is about double, -3 dB is about half, +10 dB is ten times, and -10 dB is one tenth.
topic_lessons.memorize: Memorize formulas, unit prefixes, and how quantities move when frequency or circuit values change.
topic_lessons.example: A 50 W signal increased by 3 dB is about 100 W.
topic_lessons.trap: dB is a ratio, not a direct watt value by itself.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain ratios, logarithms, decibels without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Introduction to inductance and capacitance
topic_lessons.plain_language: Inductors and capacitors oppose AC in frequency-dependent ways. Inductive reactance rises with frequency; capacitive reactance falls with frequency. Resonance occurs when they balance.
topic_lessons.memorize: Memorize formulas, unit prefixes, and how quantities move when frequency or circuit values change.
topic_lessons.example: Increasing frequency makes an inductor oppose current more, but a capacitor oppose current less.
topic_lessons.trap: Inductive and capacitive reactance move in opposite directions.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain introduction to inductance and capacitance without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Introduction to reactance and impedance
topic_lessons.plain_language: Inductors and capacitors oppose AC in frequency-dependent ways. Inductive reactance rises with frequency; capacitive reactance falls with frequency. Resonance occurs when they balance.
topic_lessons.memorize: Memorize formulas, unit prefixes, and how quantities move when frequency or circuit values change.
topic_lessons.example: Increasing frequency makes an inductor oppose current more, but a capacitor oppose current less.
topic_lessons.trap: Inductive and capacitive reactance move in opposite directions.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain introduction to reactance and impedance without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Introduction to magnetics and transformers
topic_lessons.plain_language: This topic focuses on introduction to magnetics and transformers. For electronics theory questions, write the relationship first, keep units straight, and check whether the question asks for voltage, current, resistance, power, frequency, ratio, or a measurement concept.
topic_lessons.memorize: Memorize formulas, unit prefixes, and how quantities move when frequency or circuit values change.

topic_lessons.trap: Distractors often sound familiar but answer a different topic.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain introduction to magnetics and transformers without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.plain_language: Inductors and capacitors oppose AC in frequency-dependent ways. Inductive reactance rises with frequency; capacitive reactance falls with frequency. Resonance occurs when they balance.

modules.title: Feedlines and Antenna Systems
modules.description: Transmission lines, SWR, impedance matching and common antenna types.

sections.title: Feedlines and matching
sections.content: A feedline transfers RF between the transmitter and antenna. Coaxial cable is unbalanced and commonly 50 ohms in amateur stations. Open-wire and ladder line are balanced. A balun connects balanced and unbalanced systems. High SWR indicates a mismatch; it does not mean power disappears, but it can increase line loss and stress equipment. An antenna tuner matches impedances so the transmitter sees a suitable load.

sections.title: Wavelength and antenna length
sections.content: Wavelength in metres is approximately 300 divided by frequency in MHz. A half-wave dipole is roughly one-half wavelength long overall, and each side is about one-quarter wavelength. Real antennas are shortened by end effects and environment, so exam answers often use approximate textbook relationships.

fundamentals.heading: How to think about antennas and feedlines
fundamentals.body: Antennas and feedlines are about moving RF efficiently from the transmitter into space. Keep the ideas separate: feedline type, characteristic impedance, line loss, SWR, matching, polarization, wavelength, gain, and radiation pattern are related but not identical. SWR describes mismatch; it does not by itself tell you the antenna is good or bad in every way.
fundamentals.example: Wavelength in metres is approximately 300 divided by frequency in MHz. A half-wave antenna is about half of that length.
fundamentals.trap: A tuner can make the transmitter see a better load, but it does not remove all feedline loss or magically fix the antenna.

topic_lessons.title: Feed line characteristics, characteristic impedance
topic_lessons.plain_language: This topic focuses on feed line characteristics, characteristic impedance. For antenna and feedline questions, keep impedance, loss, wavelength, polarization, radiation pattern, matching, and standing-wave behaviour separate.
topic_lessons.memorize: Keep feedline, matching, wavelength, polarization, gain, and antenna type separate.
topic_lessons.example: Before answering, restate the question in your own words and identify the one concept being tested.
topic_lessons.trap: Distractors often sound familiar but answer a different topic.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain feed line characteristics, characteristic impedance without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Balanced and unbalanced feed lines, baluns
topic_lessons.plain_language: Balanced lines have equal and opposite conductors. Coax is unbalanced. A balun helps connect balanced and unbalanced systems.
topic_lessons.memorize: Keep feedline, matching, wavelength, polarization, gain, and antenna type separate.
topic_lessons.example: A dipole fed with coax often uses a balun at the feed point.
topic_lessons.trap: Do not call every feedline coaxial cable.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain balanced and unbalanced feed lines, baluns without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Popular antenna feed line and coaxial connector types
topic_lessons.plain_language: This topic focuses on popular antenna feed line and coaxial connector types. For antenna and feedline questions, keep impedance, loss, wavelength, polarization, radiation pattern, matching, and standing-wave behaviour separate.

topic_lessons.title: Standing waves, standing wave ratio meter
topic_lessons.plain_language: SWR indicates mismatch between feedline and load. Matching makes the transmitter see a suitable impedance.
topic_lessons.memorize: Keep feedline, matching, wavelength, polarization, gain, and antenna type separate.
topic_lessons.example: A tuner can reduce the SWR seen by the transmitter.
topic_lessons.trap: A low SWR reading does not prove the antenna radiates efficiently.

topic_lessons.title: Wavelength vs physical length
topic_lessons.plain_language: Wavelength is the physical length of one RF cycle. In metres, approximate wavelength is 300 divided by frequency in MHz.
topic_lessons.memorize: Keep feedline, matching, wavelength, polarization, gain, and antenna type separate.
topic_lessons.example: At 150 MHz, wavelength is about 300 / 150 = 2 m.
topic_lessons.trap: A half-wave antenna is half the wavelength overall, not the full wavelength.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain wavelength vs physical length without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Gain, directivity, radiation pattern, antenna bandwidth
topic_lessons.plain_language: Gain and directivity describe where energy is concentrated. A directional antenna sends and receives better in some directions.
topic_lessons.memorize: Keep feedline, matching, wavelength, polarization, gain, and antenna type separate.
topic_lessons.example: A Yagi has a driven element plus parasitic elements that shape the pattern.
topic_lessons.trap: Gain does not create power; it concentrates radiation.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain gain, directivity, radiation pattern, antenna bandwidth without looking at the answer choices?

modules.title: Radio Wave Propagation
modules.description: How radio waves move through ground, air and ionosphere.

sections.title: Choosing the propagation model
sections.content: Line-of-sight dominates VHF and UHF. Ground wave follows the earth but is strongest at lower frequencies. Sky wave uses the ionosphere and is central to HF. The D layer absorbs, especially during daylight. E and F layers can refract signals. Solar activity, time of day, season and frequency determine whether a band is open.

sections.title: High-yield terms
points: Skip zone: area between the end of ground-wave coverage and the first sky-wave return.
MUF: maximum usable frequency for a path.
Critical frequency: highest frequency returned vertically by an ionospheric layer.
Sporadic-E: VHF propagation caused by dense E-layer patches.
Aurora: VHF propagation using ionized auroral regions, often distorted.
Ducting: VHF/UHF propagation through atmospheric layers.

fundamentals.heading: How to think about propagation
fundamentals.body: Propagation questions ask how a signal gets from one station to another. First identify the frequency range and path. VHF/UHF is usually line-of-sight unless a special mode is named. HF often uses sky wave and the ionosphere. The D layer mainly absorbs, while E and F layers can refract signals. Solar activity, time of day, season, and frequency decide which paths are open.
fundamentals.example: A skip zone is the area beyond ground-wave coverage but before the first sky-wave signal returns to earth.
fundamentals.trap: Do not use one propagation rule for every band. HF, VHF, and UHF behave differently.

topic_lessons.title: Line of sight, ground wave, ionospheric wave
topic_lessons.plain_language: Line-of-sight signals travel mostly directly and are common at VHF/UHF. Ground wave follows the earth and is strongest at lower frequencies.
topic_lessons.memorize: Identify frequency range, path type, ionospheric layer, and solar condition.
topic_lessons.example: A local VHF repeater contact is usually line-of-sight or near line-of-sight.
topic_lessons.trap: Do not expect ordinary VHF/UHF to behave like HF sky wave.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain line of sight, ground wave, ionospheric wave without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Ionosphere and ionospheric regions
topic_lessons.plain_language: HF signals can refract from ionospheric layers and return to earth. One return is a hop; the skip zone is the area before the first return.
topic_lessons.memorize: Identify frequency range, path type, ionospheric layer, and solar condition.
topic_lessons.example: The D layer tends to absorb HF, especially by day; E and F layers can return signals.
topic_lessons.trap: Absorption and refraction are different effects.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain ionosphere and ionospheric regions without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Propagation hops, skip zone, skip distance
topic_lessons.plain_language: HF signals can refract from ionospheric layers and return to earth. One return is a hop; the skip zone is the area before the first return.
topic_lessons.memorize: Identify frequency range, path type, ionospheric layer, and solar condition.
topic_lessons.example: The D layer tends to absorb HF, especially by day; E and F layers can return signals.
topic_lessons.trap: Absorption and refraction are different effects.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain propagation hops, skip zone, skip distance without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Ionospheric absorption, causes and variation, fading, phase shift, Faraday rotation
topic_lessons.plain_language: This topic focuses on ionospheric absorption, causes and variation, fading, phase shift, faraday rotation. For propagation questions, first identify the frequency range and path: line of sight, ground wave, sky wave, ionospheric layer, solar condition, or special VHF/UHF mode.
topic_lessons.memorize: Identify frequency range, path type, ionospheric layer, and solar condition.
topic_lessons.example: Before answering, restate the question in your own words and identify the one concept being tested.
topic_lessons.trap: Distractors often sound familiar but answer a different topic.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain ionospheric absorption, causes and variation, fading, phase shift, faraday rotation without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Solar activity, sunspots, sunspot cycle
topic_lessons.plain_language: Solar activity changes ionization, which changes what frequencies can travel by sky wave. MUF is the maximum usable frequency for a path.
topic_lessons.memorize: Identify frequency range, path type, ionospheric layer, and solar condition.
topic_lessons.example: Higher solar activity can improve higher-frequency HF propagation.
topic_lessons.trap: MUF is path-dependent, not a fixed value for everyone everywhere.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain solar activity, sunspots, sunspot cycle without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: MF and HF, critical and maximum usable frequencies, solar flux
topic_lessons.plain_language: Solar activity changes ionization, which changes what frequencies can travel by sky wave. MUF is the maximum usable frequency for a path.
topic_lessons.memorize: Identify frequency range, path type, ionospheric layer, and solar condition.
topic_lessons.example: Higher solar activity can improve higher-frequency HF propagation.
topic_lessons.trap: MUF is path-dependent, not a fixed value for everyone everywhere.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain mf and hf, critical and maximum usable frequencies, solar flux without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: VHF and UHF, sporadic-E, aurora, ducting
topic_lessons.plain_language: Special VHF/UHF propagation modes include sporadic-E, aurora, ducting, and scatter. They are exceptions to normal line-of-sight expectations.
topic_lessons.memorize: Identify frequency range, path type, ionospheric layer, and solar condition.
topic_lessons.example: Ducting can carry VHF/UHF signals farther than normal through atmospheric layers.
topic_lessons.trap: The special mode named in the question usually points directly to the answer.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain vhf and uhf, sporadic-e, aurora, ducting without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Scatter: HF, VHF, UHF
topic_lessons.plain_language: Special VHF/UHF propagation modes include sporadic-E, aurora, ducting, and scatter. They are exceptions to normal line-of-sight expectations.
topic_lessons.memorize: Identify frequency range, path type, ionospheric layer, and solar condition.
topic_lessons.example: Ducting can carry VHF/UHF signals farther than normal through atmospheric layers.
topic_lessons.trap: The special mode named in the question usually points directly to the answer.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain scatter: hf, vhf, uhf without looking at the answer choices?

modules.title: Interference and Suppression
modules.description: How interference is created, diagnosed and reduced.

sections.title: Diagnosing interference
sections.content: Identify whether the unwanted signal is caused by the receiver, the transmitter, or external wiring acting as an antenna. Front-end overload and cross-modulation happen in receivers. Audio rectification happens when RF is detected in audio wiring. Harmonics and spurious emissions come from transmitters. Splatter is often caused by overmodulation or overdriven stages.

sections.title: Filter choices
rows: Passes lower frequencies, attenuates higher frequencies; common at transmitter outputs to reduce harmonics.
Passes higher frequencies, attenuates lower frequencies; can help protect TV receivers from HF energy.
Passes a selected range and rejects energy outside it.
Rejects a selected frequency or range while passing others.

fundamentals.heading: How to think about interference
fundamentals.body: Interference questions are troubleshooting questions. Decide whether the problem is caused by the transmitter, the receiver, or nearby wiring acting like an antenna. Receiver overload and cross-modulation happen in receivers. Audio rectification happens when RF is detected in audio wiring. Harmonics, spurious emissions, key clicks, and splatter come from transmitter or signal-quality problems.
fundamentals.example: A low-pass filter at a transmitter output is a common way to reduce harmonic energy above the desired signal.
fundamentals.trap: Do not assume every interference problem is caused by transmitter harmonics. Receiver overload and audio rectification are common exam distinctions.

topic_lessons.title: Front-end overload, cross-modulation
topic_lessons.plain_language: Front-end overload happens when strong signals overwhelm receiver input stages. Cross-modulation is unwanted modulation transferred from one signal to another in an overloaded receiver.
topic_lessons.memorize: Diagnose the source first, then choose the cure: receiver overload, audio rectification, transmitter emissions, or filtering.
topic_lessons.example: Adding attenuation or filtering before the receiver can help overload.
topic_lessons.trap: This is a receiver problem, not always a transmitter harmonic problem.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain front-end overload, cross-modulation without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Audio rectification, bypass capacitors, ferrites
topic_lessons.plain_language: Audio rectification happens when RF gets into audio wiring and is detected like a crude radio receiver. Bypass capacitors and ferrites can reduce RF on leads.
topic_lessons.memorize: Diagnose the source first, then choose the cure: receiver overload, audio rectification, transmitter emissions, or filtering.
topic_lessons.example: A telephone or speaker picking up RF can be an audio rectification symptom.
topic_lessons.trap: The transmitter may be clean even when nearby audio gear is poorly immune.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain audio rectification, bypass capacitors, ferrites without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Intermodulation, spurious emissions, key clicks
topic_lessons.plain_language: These are unwanted transmitter outputs or signal defects. Harmonics are multiples of the wanted frequency. Splatter often comes from overdriving or overmodulation.
topic_lessons.memorize: Diagnose the source first, then choose the cure: receiver overload, audio rectification, transmitter emissions, or filtering.
topic_lessons.example: A low-pass filter helps reduce harmonics above the intended signal.
topic_lessons.trap: Do not use a receiver-overload cure for a dirty transmitter signal.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain intermodulation, spurious emissions, key clicks without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Harmonics, splatter, transmitter adjustments
topic_lessons.plain_language: These are unwanted transmitter outputs or signal defects. Harmonics are multiples of the wanted frequency. Splatter often comes from overdriving or overmodulation.
topic_lessons.memorize: Diagnose the source first, then choose the cure: receiver overload, audio rectification, transmitter emissions, or filtering.
topic_lessons.example: A low-pass filter helps reduce harmonics above the intended signal.
topic_lessons.trap: Do not use a receiver-overload cure for a dirty transmitter signal.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain harmonics, splatter, transmitter adjustments without looking at the answer choices?

topic_lessons.title: Use of filters: low-pass, high-pass, band-pass, band-reject
topic_lessons.plain_language: Filters pass wanted frequencies and reduce unwanted ones. Low-pass passes low frequencies, high-pass passes high frequencies, band-pass passes a range, and notch rejects a range.
topic_lessons.memorize: Diagnose the source first, then choose the cure: receiver overload, audio rectification, transmitter emissions, or filtering.
topic_lessons.example: A band-reject or notch filter can reduce one troublesome frequency range.
topic_lessons.trap: Pick the filter by what it passes or rejects, not by its name alone.
topic_lessons.check: Can you explain use of filters: low-pass, high-pass, band-pass, band-reject without looking at the answer choices?
